1.1.1 Vector Operations\nIf you walk.text:
4 miles due north and then 3 miles due east (Fig. 1.1), you will have
gone a total of 7 miles, but you‚Äôre not 7 miles from where you set out‚ÄĒyou‚Äôre
only 5. We need an arithmetic to describe quantities like this, which evidently do
not add in the ordinary way. The reason they don‚Äôt, of course, is that displace-
ments (straight line segments going from one point to another) have direction
as well as magnitude (length), and it is essential to take both into account when
you combine them. Such objects are called vectors: velocity, acceleration, force
and momentum are other examples. By contrast, quantities that have magnitude
but no direction are called scalars: examples include mass, charge, density, and
temperature.
I shall use boldface (A, B, and so on) for vectors and ordinary type for scalars.
The magnitude of a vector A is written |A| or, more simply, A. In diagrams, vec-
tors are denoted by arrows: the length of the arrow is proportional to the magni-
tude of the vector, and the arrowhead indicates its direction. Minus A (‚ąíA) is a
vector with the same magnitude as A but of opposite direction (Fig. 1.2). Note that
vectors have magnitude and direction but not location: a displacement of 4 miles
due north from Washington is represented by the same vector as a displacement 4
miles north from Baltimore (neglecting, of course, the curvature of the earth). On
a diagram, therefore, you can slide the arrow around at will, as long as you don‚Äôt
change its length or direction.
We deÔ¨Āne four vector operations: addition and three kinds of multiplication.

1.1.5 How Vectors Transform.text:
2
The deÔ¨Ānition of a vector as ‚Äúa quantity with a magnitude and direction‚ÄĚ is not
altogether satisfactory: What precisely does ‚Äúdirection‚ÄĚ mean? This may seem a
pedantic question, but we shall soon encounter a species of derivative that looks
rather like a vector, and we‚Äôll want to know for sure whether it is one.
You might be inclined to say that a vector is anything that has three components
that combine properly under addition. Well, how about this: We have a barrel of
fruit that contains Nx pears, Ny apples, and Nz bananas. Is N = Nx ňÜx + Ny ňÜy +
NzňÜz a vector? It has three components, and when you add another barrel with
Mx pears, My apples, and Mz bananas the result is (Nx + Mx) pears, (Ny + My)
apples, (Nz + Mz) bananas. So it does add like a vector. Yet it‚Äôs obviously not
a vector, in the physicist‚Äôs sense of the word, because it doesn‚Äôt really have a
direction. What exactly is wrong with it?
The answer is that N does not transform properly when you change coordi-
nates. The coordinate frame we use to describe positions in space is of course
entirely arbitrary, but there is a speciÔ¨Āc geometrical transformation law for con-
verting vector components from one frame to another. Suppose, for instance, the
x, y, z system is rotated by angle ŌÜ, relative to x, y, z, about the common x = x
axes. From Fig. 1.15,

1.2 DIFFERENTIAL CALCULUS.text:
1.2.1
‚ÄúOrdinary‚ÄĚ Derivatives
Suppose we have a function of one variable: f (x). Question: What does the
derivative, d f/dx, do for us? Answer: It tells us how rapidly the function f (x)
varies when we change the argument x by a tiny amount, dx:
d f =
d f
dx

dx.
(1.33)
In words: If we increment x by an inÔ¨Ānitesimal amount dx, then f changes
by an amount d f ; the derivative is the proportionality factor. For example, in
Fig. 1.17(a), the function varies slowly with x, and the derivative is correspond-
ingly small. In Fig. 1.17(b), f increases rapidly with x, and the derivative is large,
as you move away from x = 0.
Geometrical Interpretation: The derivative d f/dx is the slope of the graph of
f versus x.

1.2.2 Gradient\nSuppose.text:
, now, that we have a function of three variables‚ÄĒsay, the temperature
T (x, y, z) in this room. (Start out in one corner, and set up a system of axes; then
for each point (x, y, z) in the room, T gives the temperature at that spot.) We want
to generalize the notion of ‚Äúderivative‚ÄĚ to functions like T , which depend not on
one but on three variables.
A derivative is supposed to tell us how fast the function varies, if we move a
little distance. But this time the situation is more complicated, because it depends
on what direction we move: If we go straight up, then the temperature will prob-
ably increase fairly rapidly, but if we move horizontally, it may not change much
at all. In fact, the question ‚ÄúHow fast does T vary?‚ÄĚ has an inÔ¨Ānite number of
answers, one for each direction we might choose to explore.
Fortunately, the problem is not as bad as it looks. A theorem on partial deriva-
tives states that

Of course, del is not a vector, in the usual sense. Indeed, it doesn‚Äôt mean much
until we provide it with a function to act upon. Furthermore, it does not ‚Äúmultiply‚ÄĚ
T ; rather, it is an instruction to differentiate what follows. To be precise, then, we
say that ‚ąáis a vector operator that acts upon T , not a vector that multiplies T .
With this qualiÔ¨Ācation, though, ‚ąámimics the behavior of an ordinary vector in
virtually every way; almost anything that can be done with other vectors can also
be done with ‚ąá, if we merely translate ‚Äúmultiply‚ÄĚ by ‚Äúact upon.‚ÄĚ So by all means
take the vector appearance of ‚ąáseriously: it is a marvelous piece of notational
simpliÔ¨Ācation, as you will appreciate if you ever consult Maxwell‚Äôs original work
on electromagnetism, written without the beneÔ¨Āt of ‚ąá.
Now, an ordinary vector A can multiply in three ways:
1. By a scalar a : Aa;
2. By a vector B, via the dot product: A ¬∑ B;
3. By a vector B via the cross product: A √ó B.
Correspondingly, there are three ways the operator ‚ąácan act:
1. On a scalar function T : ‚ąáT (the gradient);
2. On a vector function v, via the dot product: ‚ąá¬∑ v (the divergence);
3. On a vector function v, via the cross product: ‚ąá√ó v (the curl).

Geometrical Interpretation: The name divergence is well chosen, for ‚ąá¬∑ v
is a measure of how much the vector v spreads out (diverges) from the point in
question. For example, the vector function in Fig. 1.18a has a large (positive)
divergence (if the arrows pointed in, it would be a negative divergence), the func-
tion in Fig. 1.18b has zero divergence, and the function in Fig. 1.18c again has a
positive divergence. (Please understand that v here is a function‚ÄĒthere‚Äôs a differ-
ent vector associated with every point in space. In the diagrams, of course, I can
only draw the arrows at a few representative locations.)
Imagine standing at the edge of a pond. Sprinkle some sawdust or pine needles
on the surface. If the material spreads out, then you dropped it at a point of positive
divergence; if it collects together, you dropped it at a point of negative divergence.
(The vector function v in this model is the velocity of the water at the surface‚ÄĒ
this is a two-dimensional example, but it helps give one a ‚Äúfeel‚ÄĚ for what the
divergence means. A point of positive divergence is a source, or ‚Äúfaucet‚ÄĚ; a point
of negative divergence is a sink, or ‚Äúdrain.‚ÄĚ)

Geometrical Interpretation: The name curl is also well chosen, for ‚ąá√ó v is
a measure of how much the vector v swirls around the point in question. Thus
the three functions in Fig. 1.18 all have zero curl (as you can easily check for
yourself), whereas the functions in Fig. 1.19 have a substantial curl, pointing in the
z direction, as the natural right-hand rule would suggest. Imagine (again) you are
standing at the edge of a pond. Float a small paddlewheel (a cork with toothpicks
pointing out radially would do); if it starts to rotate, then you placed it at a point
of nonzero curl. A whirlpool would be a region of large curl.

Geometrical Interpretation: According to Eq. 1.33, d f = (d f/dx)dx is the
inÔ¨Ānitesimal change in f when you go from (x) to (x + dx). The fundamental
theorem (Eq. 1.54) says that if you chop the interval from a to b (Fig. 1.25) into
many tiny pieces, dx, and add up the increments d f from each little piece, the
result is (not surprisingly) equal to the total change in f : f (b) ‚ąíf (a). In other
words, there are two ways to determine the total change in the function: either
subtract the values at the ends or go step-by-step, adding up all the tiny increments
as you go. You‚Äôll get the same answer either way.
Notice the basic format of the fundamental theorem: the integral of a derivative
over some region is given by the value of the function at the end points (bound-
aries). In vector calculus there are three species of derivative (gradient, diver-
gence, and curl), and each has its own ‚Äúfundamental theorem,‚ÄĚ with essentially
the same format. I don‚Äôt plan to prove these theorems here; rather, I will explain
what they mean, and try to make them plausible. Proofs are given in Appendix A.

As always, the integral of a derivative (here, the curl) over a region (here, a patch
of surface, S) is equal to the value of the function at the boundary (here, the
perimeter of the patch, P). As in the case of the divergence theorem, the boundary
term is itself an integral‚ÄĒspeciÔ¨Ācally, a closed line integral.
Geometrical Interpretation: Recall that the curl measures the ‚Äútwist‚ÄĚ of the
vectors v; a region of high curl is a whirlpool‚ÄĒif you put a tiny paddle wheel
there, it will rotate. Now, the integral of the curl over some surface (or, more
precisely, the Ô¨āux of the curl through that surface) represents the ‚Äútotal amount
of swirl,‚ÄĚ and we can determine that just as well by going around the edge and
Ô¨Ānding how much the Ô¨āow is following the boundary (Fig. 1.31). Indeed,

v ¬∑ dl
is sometimes called the circulation of v.
You may have noticed an apparent ambiguity in Stokes‚Äô theorem: concerning
the boundary line integral, which way are we supposed to go around (clockwise
or counterclockwise)? If we go the ‚Äúwrong‚ÄĚ way, we‚Äôll pick up an overall sign
error. The answer is that it doesn‚Äôt matter which way you go as long as you are
consistent, for there is a compensating sign ambiguity in the surface integral:
Which way does da point? For a closed surface (as in the divergence theorem),
da points in the direction of the outward normal; but for an open surface, which
way is ‚Äúout‚ÄĚ? Consistency in Stokes‚Äô theorem (as in all such matters) is given by
the right-hand rule: if your Ô¨Āngers point in the direction of the line integral, then
your thumb Ô¨Āxes the direction of da (Fig. 1.32).
Now, there are plenty of surfaces (inÔ¨Ānitely many) that share any given bound-
ary line. Twist a paper clip into a loop, and dip it in soapy water. The soap Ô¨Ālm
constitutes a surface, with the wire loop as its boundary. If you blow on it, the soap
Ô¨Ālm will expand, making a larger surface, with the same boundary. Ordinarily, a
Ô¨āux integral depends critically on what surface you integrate over, but evidently

1.46 Technically.text:
, őī(x) is not a function at all, since its value is not Ô¨Ānite at x = 0; in the
mathematical literature it is known as a generalized function, or distribution. It
is, if you like, the limit of a sequence of functions, such as rectangles Rn(x), of
height n and width 1/n, or isosceles triangles Tn(x), of height n and base 2/n
(Fig. 1.46).
If f (x) is some ‚Äúordinary‚ÄĚ function (that is, not another delta function‚ÄĒin
fact, just to be on the safe side, let‚Äôs say that f (x) is continuous), then the product
f (x)őī(x) is zero everywhere except at x = 0. It follows that
f (x)őī(x) = f (0)őī(x).
(1.88)
(This is the most important fact about the delta function, so make sure you under-
stand why it is true: since the product is zero anyway except at x = 0, we may as
well replace f (x) by the value it assumes at the origin.) In particular
  ‚ąě
‚ąí‚ąě
f (x)őī(x) dx = f (0)
  ‚ąě
‚ąí‚ąě
őī(x) dx = f (0).
(1.89)
Under an integral, then, the delta function ‚Äúpicks out‚ÄĚ the value of f (x) at x = 0.
(Here and below, the integral need not run from ‚ąí‚ąěto +‚ąě; it is sufÔ¨Ācient that
the domain extend across the delta function, and ‚ąíŌĶ to +ŌĶ would do as well.)

2.1.1 Introduction\nThe fundamental prob.text:
(Fig. 2.1): We
have some electric charges, q1, q2, q3, . . . (call them source charges); what force
do they exert on another charge, Q (call it the test charge)? The positions of the
source charges are given (as functions of time); the trajectory of the test particle
is to be calculated. In general, both the source charges and the test charge are in
motion.
The solution to this problem is facilitated by the principle of superposition,
which states that the interaction between any two charges is completely unaffected
by the presence of others. This means that to determine the force on Q, we can Ô¨Ārst
compute the force F1, due to q1 alone (ignoring all the others); then we compute
the force F2, due to q2 alone; and so on. Finally, we take the vector sum of all
these individual forces: F = F1 + F2 + F3 + . . . Thus, if we can Ô¨Ānd the force
on Q due to a single source charge q, we are, in principle, done (the rest is just a
question of repeating the same operation over and over, and adding it all up).1
Well, at Ô¨Ārst sight this looks very easy: Why don‚Äôt I just write down the formula
for the force on Q due to q, and be done with it? I could, and in Chapter 10 I
shall, but you would be shocked to see it at this stage, for not only does the force
on Q depend on the separation distance r between the charges (Fig. 2.2), it also

2.1.2 Coulomb.text:
‚Äôs Law
What is the force on a test charge Q due to a single point charge q, that is at rest a
distance r away? The answer (based on experiments) is given by Coulomb‚Äôs law:
F =

The constant ŌĶ0 is called (ludicrously) the permittivity of free space. In SI units,
where force is in newtons (N), distance in meters (m), and charge in coulombs (C),

In words, the force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely
proportional to the square of the separation distance. As always (Sect. 1.1.4), r is
the separation vector from r‚Ä≤ (the location of q) to r (the location of Q):
r = r ‚ąír‚Ä≤;
(2.2)

Q; it is repulsive if q and Q have the same sign, and attractive if their signs are
opposite.
Coulomb‚Äôs law and the principle of superposition constitute the physical input
for electrostatics‚ÄĒthe rest, except for some special properties of matter, is math-
ematical elaboration of these fundamental rules.

In SI units, where force is in newtons (N), distance in meters (m), and charge in coulombs (C), ŌĶ0 = 8.85 √ó 10‚ąí12 C2 N ¬∑ m2 . In words, the force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance. As always (Sect. 1.1.4), r is the separation vector from r‚Ä≤ (the location of q) to r (the location of Q): r = r ‚ąír‚Ä≤; (2.2) r is its magnitude, and ňÜr is its direction. The force points along
related_equations.2.2: experiments) is given by Coulomb‚Äôs law: F = 1 4ŌÄŌĶ0 qQ r2 ňÜr. (2.1) The constant ŌĶ0 is called (ludicrously) the permittivity of free space. In SI units, where force is in newtons (N), distance in meters (m), and charge in coulombs (C), ŌĶ0 = 8.85 √ó 10‚ąí12 C2 N ¬∑ m2 . In words, the force is proportional to the product of the charges and inversely proportional to the square of the separation distance. As always (Sect. 1.1.4), r is the separation vector from r‚Ä≤ (the location of q) to r (the location of Q): r = r ‚ąír‚Ä≤; (2.2) r is its magnitude, and ňÜr is its direction. The force points along the line from q to Q; it is repulsive if q and Q have the same sign, and attractive if their signs are opposite. Coulomb‚Äôs law and the principle of superposition constitute the physical input for electrostatics‚ÄĒthe rest, except for some special properties of matter, is math- ematical elaboration of these fundamental rules. 2.2.1 Field Lines.text:
, Flux, and Gauss‚Äôs Law
In principle, we are done with the subject of electrostatics. Equation 2.8 tells us
how to compute the Ô¨Āeld of a charge distribution, and Eq. 2.3 tells us what the
force on a charge Q placed in this Ô¨Āeld will be. Unfortunately, as you may have
discovered in working Prob. 2.7, the integrals involved in computing E can be
formidable, even for reasonably simple charge distributions. Much of the rest of
electrostatics is devoted to assembling a bag of tools and tricks for avoiding these
integrals. It all begins with the divergence and curl of E. I shall calculate the
divergence of E directly from Eq. 2.8, in Sect. 2.2.2, but Ô¨Ārst I want to show you
a more qualitative, and perhaps more illuminating, intuitive approach.
Let‚Äôs begin with the simplest possible case: a single point charge q, situated at
the origin:

Notice that it all hinges on the 1/r2 character of Coulomb‚Äôs law; without that the crucial cancellation of the r‚Äôs in Eq. 2.12  would not take place, and the total Ô¨āux of E would depend on the surface chosen, not merely on the total charge enclosed. Other 1/r2 forces (I am thinking particularly of Newton‚Äôs law of universal gravitation) will obey ‚ÄúGauss‚Äôs laws‚ÄĚ of their own, and the applications we develop here carry over directly.

2.3.1 Introduction to Potential\nThe ele.text:
Ô¨Āeld E is not just any old vector function. It is a very special kind of
vector function: one whose curl is zero. E = yňÜx, for example, could not possibly
be an electrostatic Ô¨Āeld; no set of charges, regardless of their sizes and positions,
could ever produce such a Ô¨Āeld. We‚Äôre going to exploit this special property of
electric Ô¨Āelds to reduce a vector problem (Ô¨Ānding E) to a much simpler scalar
problem. The Ô¨Ārst theorem in Sect. 1.6.2 asserts that any vector whose curl is zero
is equal to the gradient of some scalar. What I‚Äôm going to do now amounts to a
proof of that claim, in the context of electrostatics.

2.3.2 Comments on Potential.text:
(i) The name. The word ‚Äúpotential‚ÄĚ is a hideous misnomer because it inevitably
reminds you of potential energy. This is particularly insidious, because there
is a connection between ‚Äúpotential‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúpotential energy,‚ÄĚ as you will see in
Sect. 2.4. I‚Äôm sorry that it is impossible to escape this word. The best I can do is
to insist once and for all that ‚Äúpotential‚ÄĚ and ‚Äúpotential energy‚ÄĚ are completely
different terms and should, by all rights, have different names. Incidentally, a sur-
face over which the potential is constant is called an equipotential.
(ii) Advantage of the potential formulation. If you know V , you can eas-
ily get E‚ÄĒjust take the gradient: E = ‚ąí‚ąáV . This is quite extraordinary when
you stop to think about it, for E is a vector quantity (three components), but V
is a scalar (one component). How can one function possibly contain all the in-
formation that three independent functions carry? The answer is that the three
components of E are not really as independent as they look; in fact, they are ex-
plicitly interrelated by the very condition we started with, ‚ąá√ó E = 0. In terms of
components,
‚ąāEx
‚ąāy = ‚ąāEy
‚ąāx ,

‚ąāy = ‚ąāEy
‚ąāz ,

‚ąāx .
This brings us back to my observation at the beginning of Sect. 2.3.1: E is a very
special kind of vector. What the potential formulation does is to exploit this feature
to maximum advantage, reducing a vector problem to a scalar one, in which there
is no need to fuss with components.

2.4.4 Comments on Electrostatic Energy.text:
(i) A perplexing ‚Äúinconsistency.‚ÄĚ
Equation 2.45 clearly implies that the
energy of a stationary charge distribution is always positive. On the other hand,
Eq. 2.42 (from which 2.45 was in fact derived), can be positive or negative. For
instance, according to Eq. 2.42, the energy of two equal but opposite charges a
distance r apart is ‚ąí(1/4ŌÄŌĶ0)(q2/r). What‚Äôs gone wrong? Which equation is cor-
rect?
The answer is that both are correct, but they speak to slightly different ques-
tions. Equation 2.42 does not take into account the work necessary to make the
point charges in the Ô¨Ārst place; we started with point charges and simply found
the work required to bring them together. This is wise strategy, since Eq. 2.45
indicates that the energy of a point charge is in fact inÔ¨Ānite:

Equation 2.45 is more complete, in the sense that it tells you the total energy
stored in a charge conÔ¨Āguration, but Eq. 2.42 is more appropriate when you‚Äôre
dealing with point charges, because we prefer (for good reason!) to leave out that
portion of the total energy that is attributable to the fabrication of the point charges
themselves. In practice, after all, the point charges (electrons, say) are given to us
ready-made; all we do is move them around. Since we did not put them together,
and we cannot take them apart, it is immaterial how much work the process would
involve. (Still, the inÔ¨Ānite energy of a point charge is a recurring source of embar-
rassment for electromagnetic theory, afÔ¨āicting the quantum version as well as the
classical. We shall return to the problem in Chapter 11.)
Now, you may wonder where the inconsistency crept into an apparently water-
tight derivation. The ‚ÄúÔ¨āaw‚ÄĚ lies between Eqs. 2.42 and 2.43: in the former, V (ri)
represents the potential due to all the other charges but not qi, whereas in the
latter, V (r) is the full potential. For a continuous distribution, there is no distinc-
tion, since the amount of charge right at the point r is vanishingly small, and its
contribution to the potential is zero. But in the presence of point charges you‚Äôd
better stick with Eq. 2.42.
(ii) Where is the energy stored? Equations 2.43 and 2.45 offer two different
ways of calculating the same thing. The Ô¨Ārst is an integral over the charge dis-
tribution; the second is an integral over the Ô¨Āeld. These can involve completely
different regions. For instance, in the case of the spherical shell (Ex. 2.9) the
charge is conÔ¨Āned to the surface, whereas the electric Ô¨Āeld is everywhere outside

Problem 2.56 All of electrostatics follows from the 1/r 2 character of Coulomb‚Äôs
law, together with the principle of superposition. An analogous theory can therefore
be constructed for Newton‚Äôs law of universal gravitation. What is the gravitational
energy of a sphere, of mass M and radius R, assuming the density is uniform?
Use your result to estimate the gravitational energy of the sun (look up the relevant
numbers). Note that the energy is negative‚ÄĒmasses attract, whereas (like) electric
charges repel. As the matter ‚Äúfalls in,‚ÄĚ to create the sun, its energy is converted into
other forms (typically thermal), and it is subsequently released in the form of radia-
tion. The sun radiates at a rate of 3.86 √ó 1026 W; if all this came from gravitational
energy, how long would the sun last? [The sun is in fact much older than that, so
evidently this is not the source of its power.14]

2.5.1 Basic Properties\nIn an insulator.text:
, such as glass or rubber, each electron is on a short leash, attached
to a particular atom. In a metallic conductor, by contrast, one or more electrons
per atom are free to roam. (In liquid conductors such as salt water, it is ions that
do the moving.) A perfect conductor would contain an unlimited supply of free
charges. In real life there are no perfect conductors, but metals come pretty close,
for most purposes.

2.43 I think it is astonishing that the.text:
Ô¨āows to the surface.
Because of their mutual repulsion, the charges naturally spread out as much as
possible, but for all of them to go to the surface seems like a waste of the interior
space. Surely we could do better, from the point of view of making each charge
as far as possible from its neighbors, to sprinkle some of them throughout the
volume . . . Well, it simply is not so. You do best to put all the charge on the
surface, and this is true regardless of the size or shape of the conductor.10
The problem can also be phrased in terms of energy. Like any other free
dynamical system, the charge on a conductor will seek the conÔ¨Āguration that
minimizes its potential energy. What property (iii) asserts is that the electrostatic
energy of a solid object (with speciÔ¨Āed shape and total charge) is a minimum
when that charge is spread over the surface. For instance, the energy of a sphere
is (1/8ŌÄŌĶ0)(q2/R) if the charge is uniformly distributed over the surface, as we
found in Ex. 2.9, but it is greater, (3/20ŌÄŌĶ0)(q2/R), if the charge is uniformly
distributed throughout the volume (Prob. 2.34).

2.5.2 Induced Charges\nIf you hold a cha.text:
+q near an uncharged conductor (Fig. 2.44), the two will
attract one another. The reason for this is that q will pull minus charges over to
the near side and repel plus charges to the far side. (Another way to think of it
is that the charge moves around in such a way as to kill off the Ô¨Āeld of q for
points inside the conductor, where the total Ô¨Āeld must be zero.) Since the negative
induced charge is closer to q, there is a net force of attraction. (In Chapter 3 we
shall calculate this force explicitly, for the case of a spherical conductor.)
When I speak of the Ô¨Āeld, charge, or potential ‚Äúinside‚ÄĚ a conductor, I mean in
the ‚Äúmeat‚ÄĚ of the conductor; if there is some hollow cavity in the conductor, and

2.5.4 Capacitors\nSuppose we have two co.text:

We don‚Äôt know how the charge distributes itself over the two conductors, and
calculating the Ô¨Āeld would be a nightmare, if their shapes are complicated, but
this much we do know: E is proportional to Q. For E is given by Coulomb‚Äôs law:
E =

so if you double ŌĀ, you double E. [Wait a minute! How do we know that dou-
bling Q (and also ‚ąíQ) simply doubles ŌĀ? Maybe the charge moves around into
a completely different conÔ¨Āguration, quadrupling ŌĀ in some places and halving it
in others, just so the total charge on each conductor is doubled. The fact is that
this concern is unwarranted‚ÄĒdoubling Q does double ŌĀ everywhere; it doesn‚Äôt

just have to trust me.]
Since E is proportional to Q, so also is V . The constant of proportionality is

C ‚Č°Q

Capacitance is a purely geometrical quantity, determined by the sizes, shapes, and
separation of the two conductors. In SI units, C is measured in farads (F); a farad
is a coulomb-per-volt. Actually, this turns out to be inconveniently large; more
practical units are the microfarad (10‚ąí6 F) and the picofarad (10‚ąí12 F).
Notice that V is, by deÔ¨Ānition, the potential of the positive conductor less
that of the negative one; likewise, Q is the charge of the positive conductor. Ac-
cordingly, capacitance is an intrinsically positive quantity. (By the way, you will
occasionally hear someone speak of the capacitance of a single conductor. In this
case the ‚Äúsecond conductor,‚ÄĚ with the negative charge, is an imaginary spherical
shell of inÔ¨Ānite radius surrounding the one conductor. It contributes nothing to
the Ô¨Āeld, so the capacitance is given by Eq. 2.53, where V is the potential with
inÔ¨Ānity as the reference point.)

It contributes nothing to the Ô¨Āeld, so the capacitance is given by Eq. 2.53 , where V is the potential with inÔ¨Ānity as the reference point.)

3.1.5 Boundary Conditions and Uniqueness.text:
‚Äôs equation does not by itself determine V ; in addition, suitable boundary
conditions must be supplied. This raises a delicate question: What are appropriate
boundary conditions, sufÔ¨Ācient to determine the answer and yet not so strong
as to generate inconsistencies? The one-dimensional case is easy, for here the
general solution V = mx + b contains two arbitrary constants, and we therefore
require two boundary conditions. We might, for instance, specify the value of the
function at each end, or we might give the value of the function and its derivative
at one end, or the value at one end and the derivative at the other, and so on.
But we cannot get away with just the value or just the derivative at one end‚ÄĒ
this is insufÔ¨Ācient information. Nor would it do to specify the derivatives at both
ends‚ÄĒthis would either be redundant (if the two are equal) or inconsistent (if they
are not).
In two or three dimensions we are confronted by a partial differential equation,
and it is not so obvious what would constitute acceptable boundary conditions. Is
the shape of a taut rubber membrane, for instance, uniquely determined by the
frame over which it is stretched, or, like a canning jar lid, can it snap from one
stable conÔ¨Āguration to another? The answer, as I think your intuition would sug-
gest, is that V is uniquely determined by its value at the boundary (canning jars
evidently do not obey Laplace‚Äôs equation). However, other boundary conditions
can also be used (see Prob. 3.5). The proof that a proposed set of boundary condi-
tions will sufÔ¨Āce is usually presented in the form of a uniqueness theorem. There
are many such theorems for electrostatics, all sharing the same basic format‚ÄĒI‚Äôll
show you the two most useful ones.4
First uniqueness theorem: The solution to Laplace‚Äôs equation in
some volume V is uniquely determined if V is speciÔ¨Āed on the
boundary surface S.
Proof. In Fig. 3.5 I have drawn such a region and its boundary. (There could also
be ‚Äúislands‚ÄĚ inside, so long as V is given on all their surfaces; also, the outer
4I do not intend to prove the existence of solutions here‚ÄĒthat‚Äôs a much more difÔ¨Ācult job. In context,
the existence is generally clear on physical grounds.

‚Äôs equation directly, using the method of

region, and we are asked to Ô¨Ānd the potential in the interior. The basic strategy is

We‚Äôll start with Cartesian coordinates and then do spherical coordinates (I‚Äôll leave
the cylindrical case for you to tackle on your own, in Prob. 3.24).

if Q is not zero). But we‚Äôre looking for something a bit more informative than that.

If it is not at the origin, it‚Äôs no longer a pure monopole. For instance, the charge
in Fig. 3.32 has a dipole moment p = qd ňÜy, and a corresponding dipole term in
its potential. The monopole potential (1/4ŌÄŌĶ0)q/r is not quite correct for this
conÔ¨Āguration; rather, the exact potential is (1/4ŌÄŌĶ0)q/r. The multipole expansion
is, remember, a series in inverse powers of r (the distance to the origin), and when
we expand 1/r, we get all powers, not just the Ô¨Ārst.
So moving the origin (or, what amounts to the same thing, moving the charge)
can radically alter a multipole expansion. The monopole moment Q does not
change, since the total charge is obviously independent of the coordinate system.
(In Fig. 3.32, the monopole term was unaffected when we moved q away from
the origin‚ÄĒit‚Äôs just that it was no longer the whole story: a dipole term‚ÄĒand for
that matter all higher poles‚ÄĒappeared as well.) Ordinarily, the dipole moment
does change when you shift the origin, but there is an important exception: If the
total charge is zero, then the dipole moment is independent of the choice of origin.

gradient introduces another factor of 1/r.)

3.39 Problem.text:
3.41 Buckminsterfullerine is a molecule of 60 carbon atoms arranged
like the stitching on a soccer-ball. It may be approximated as a conducting spher-
ical shell of radius R = 3.5 √Ö. A nearby electron would be attracted, according to
Prob. 3.9, so it is not surprising that the ion C‚ąí
60 exists. (Imagine that the electron‚ÄĒ
on average‚ÄĒsmears itself out uniformly over the surface.) But how about a second
electron? At large distances it would be repelled by the ion, obviously, but at a cer-
tain distance r (from the center), the net force is zero, and closer than this it would
be attracted. So an electron with enough energy to get in that close should bind.
(a) Find r, in √Ö. [You‚Äôll have to do it numerically.]
(b) How much energy (in electron volts) would it take to push an electron in (from
inÔ¨Ānity) to the point r?
[Incidentally, the C‚ąí‚ąí
60 ion has been observed.]21

4.1.1 Dielectrics\nIn this chapter.text:
, we shall study electric Ô¨Āelds in matter. Matter, of course, comes
in many varieties‚ÄĒsolids, liquids, gases, metals, woods, glasses‚ÄĒand these sub-
stances do not all respond in the same way to electrostatic Ô¨Āelds. Nevertheless,
most everyday objects belong (at least, in good approximation) to one of two large
classes: conductors and insulators (or dielectrics). We have already talked about
conductors; these are substances that contain an ‚Äúunlimited‚ÄĚ supply of charges
that are free to move about through the material. In practice, what this ordinarily
means is that many of the electrons (one or two per atom, in a typical metal) are
not associated with any particular nucleus, but roam around at will. In dielectrics,
by contrast, all charges are attached to speciÔ¨Āc atoms or molecules‚ÄĒthey‚Äôre on
a tight leash, and all they can do is move a bit within the atom or molecule. Such
microscopic displacements are not as dramatic as the wholesale rearrangement
of charge in a conductor, but their cumulative effects account for the characteris-
tic behavior of dielectric materials. There are actually two principal mechanisms
by which electric Ô¨Āelds can distort the charge distribution of a dielectric atom
or molecule: stretching and rotating. In the next two sections I‚Äôll discuss these
processes.

4.1.2 Induced Dipoles\nWhat happens to a.text:
Ô¨Āeld E? Your
Ô¨Ārst guess might well be: ‚ÄúAbsolutely nothing‚ÄĒsince the atom is not charged, the
Ô¨Āeld has no effect on it.‚ÄĚ But that is incorrect. Although the atom as a whole is
electrically neutral, there is a positively charged core (the nucleus) and a nega-
tively charged electron cloud surrounding it. These two regions of charge within
the atom are inÔ¨āuenced by the Ô¨Āeld: the nucleus is pushed in the direction of the
Ô¨Āeld, and the electrons the opposite way. In principle, if the Ô¨Āeld is large enough,
it can pull the atom apart completely, ‚Äúionizing‚ÄĚ it (the substance then becomes
a conductor). With less extreme Ô¨Āelds, however, an equilibrium is soon estab-
lished, for if the center of the electron cloud does not coincide with the nucleus,
these positive and negative charges attract one another, and that holds the atom
together. The two opposing forces‚ÄĒE pulling the electrons and nucleus apart,
their mutual attraction drawing them back together‚ÄĒreach a balance, leaving the

4.1.4 Polarization\nIn the previous two.text:
, we have considered the effect of an external elec-
tric Ô¨Āeld on an individual atom or molecule. We are now in a position to answer
(qualitatively) the original question: What happens to a piece of dielectric material
when it is placed in an electric Ô¨Āeld? If the substance consists of neutral atoms (or
nonpolar molecules), the Ô¨Āeld will induce in each a tiny dipole moment, pointing
in the same direction as the Ô¨Āeld.3 If the material is made up of polar molecules,
each permanent dipole will experience a torque, tending to line it up along the
Ô¨Āeld direction. (Random thermal motions compete with this process, so the align-
ment is never complete, especially at higher temperatures, and disappears almost
at once when the Ô¨Āeld is removed.)
Notice that these two mechanisms produce the same basic result: a lot of little
dipoles pointing along the direction of the Ô¨Āeld‚ÄĒthe material becomes polarized.
A convenient measure of this effect is
P ‚Č°dipole moment per unit volume,
which is called the polarization. From now on we shall not worry much about
how the polarization got there. Actually, the two mechanisms I described are
not as clear-cut as I tried to pretend. Even in polar molecules there will be

4.2.2 Physical Interpretation of Bound C.text:
Ô¨Āeld of a polarized object is identical to
the Ô¨Āeld that would be produced by a certain distribution of ‚Äúbound charges,‚ÄĚ ŌÉb
and ŌĀb. But this conclusion emerged in the course of abstract manipulations on
the integral in Eq. 4.9, and left us with no clue as to the physical meaning of these
bound charges. Indeed, some authors give you the impression that bound charges
are in some sense ‚ÄúÔ¨Āctitious‚ÄĚ‚ÄĒmere bookkeeping devices used to facilitate the
calculation of Ô¨Āelds. Nothing could be further from the truth: ŌĀb and ŌÉb repre-
sent perfectly genuine accumulations of charge. In this section I‚Äôll explain how
polarization leads to these charge distributions.
The basic idea is very simple: Suppose we have a long string of dipoles, as
shown in Fig. 4.11. Along the line, the head of one effectively cancels the tail of
its neighbor, but at the ends there are two charges left over: plus at the right end
and minus at the left. It is as if we had peeled off an electron at one end and carried
it all the way down to the other end, though in fact no single electron made the
whole trip‚ÄĒa lot of tiny displacements add up to one large one. We call the net
charge at the ends a bound charge to remind ourselves that it cannot be removed;

4
I have been sloppy about the distinction between ‚Äúpure‚ÄĚ dipoles and ‚Äúphysical‚ÄĚ
dipoles. In developing the theory of bound charges, I assumed we were working
with the pure kind‚ÄĒindeed, I started with Eq. 4.8, the formula for the potential
of a perfect dipole. And yet, an actual polarized dielectric consists of physical
dipoles, albeit extremely tiny ones. What is more, I presumed to represent dis-
crete molecular dipoles by a continuous density function P. How can I justify
this method? Outside the dielectric there is no real problem: here we are far away
from the molecules (r is many times greater than the separation distance between
plus and minus charges), so the dipole potential dominates overwhelmingly and
the detailed ‚Äúgraininess‚ÄĚ of the source is blurred by distance. Inside the dielectric,
however, we can hardly pretend to be far from all the dipoles, and the procedure I
used in Sect. 4.2.1 is open to serious challenge.
In fact, when you stop to think about it, the electric Ô¨Āeld inside matter must
be fantastically complicated, on the microscopic level. If you happen to be very
near an electron, the Ô¨Āeld is gigantic, whereas a short distance away it may be
small or may point in a totally different direction. Moreover, an instant later, as
the atoms move about, the Ô¨Āeld will have altered entirely. This true microscopic
Ô¨Āeld would be utterly impossible to calculate, nor would it be of much interest
if you could. Just as, for macroscopic purposes, we regard water as a continu-
ous Ô¨āuid, ignoring its molecular structure, so also we can ignore the microscopic

It may appear to you that I left out the surface bound charge ŌÉb in deriving
Eq. 4.22, and in a sense that is true. We cannot apply Gauss‚Äôs law precisely at the
surface of a dielectric, for here ŌĀb blows up,6 taking the divergence of E with it.
But everywhere else the logic is sound, and in fact if we picture the edge of the
dielectric as having some Ô¨Ānite thickness, within which the polarization tapers
off to zero (probably a more realistic model than an abrupt cut-off anyway), then
there is no surface bound charge; ŌĀb varies rapidly but smoothly within this ‚Äúskin,‚ÄĚ
and Gauss‚Äôs law can be safely applied everywhere. At any rate, the integral form
(Eq. 4.23) is free from this ‚Äúdefect.‚ÄĚ

4.3.1 Gauss.text:
‚Äôs Law in the Presence of Dielectrics
In Sect. 4.2 we found that the effect of polarization is to produce accumulations of
(bound) charge, ŌĀb = ‚ąí‚ąá¬∑ P within the dielectric and ŌÉb = P ¬∑ ňÜn on the surface.
The Ô¨Āeld due to polarization of the medium is just the Ô¨Āeld of this bound charge.
We are now ready to put it all together: the Ô¨Āeld attributable to bound charge plus
the Ô¨Āeld due to everything else (which, for want of a better term, we call free
charge, ŌĀ f ). The free charge might consist of electrons on a conductor or ions
embedded in the dielectric material or whatever; any charge, in other words, that
is not a result of polarization. Within the dielectric, the total charge density can
be written:
ŌĀ = ŌĀb + ŌĀ f ,
(4.20)
and Gauss‚Äôs law reads
ŌĶ0‚ąá¬∑ E = ŌĀ = ŌĀb + ŌĀ f = ‚ąí‚ąá¬∑ P + ŌĀ f ,
where E is now the total Ô¨Āeld, not just that portion generated by polarization.

4.22 looks just like Gauss‚Äôs law, only the total charge density ŌĀ is

reason, you may be tempted to conclude that D is ‚Äújust like‚ÄĚ E (apart from the
factor ŌĶ0), except that its source is ŌĀ f instead of ŌĀ: ‚ÄúTo solve problems involving

4.4.1 Susceptibility.text:
, Permittivity, Dielectric Constant
In Sects. 4.2 and 4.3 we did not commit ourselves as to the cause of P; we dealt
only with the effects of polarization. From the qualitative discussion of Sect. 4.1,
though, we know that the polarization of a dielectric ordinarily results from an
electric Ô¨Āeld, which lines up the atomic or molecular dipoles. For many sub-
stances, in fact, the polarization is proportional to the Ô¨Āeld, provided E is not
too strong:
P = ŌĶ0ŌáeE.
(4.30)

Example 4.5.
A metal sphere of radius a carries a charge Q (Fig. 4.20). It is
surrounded, out to radius b, by linear dielectric material of permittivity ŌĶ. Find

4.4.4 Forces on Dielectrics\nJust as a c.text:
Ô¨Āeld (Eq. 2.51), so too is a
dielectric‚ÄĒand for essentially the same reason: the bound charge tends to accu-
mulate near the free charge of the opposite sign. But the calculation of forces on
dielectrics can be surprisingly tricky. Consider, for example, the case of a slab of
linear dielectric material, partially inserted between the plates of a parallel-plate
capacitor (Fig. 4.30). We have always pretended that the Ô¨Āeld is uniform inside a
parallel-plate capacitor, and zero outside. If this were literally true, there would
be no net force on the dielectric at all, since the Ô¨Āeld everywhere would be per-
pendicular to the plates. However, there is in reality a fringing Ô¨Āeld around the
edges, which for most purposes can be ignored but in this case is responsible for
the whole effect. (Indeed, the Ô¨Āeld could not terminate abruptly at the edge of
the capacitor, for if it did, the line integral of E around the closed loop shown in
Fig. 4.31 would not be zero.) It is this nonuniform fringing Ô¨Āeld that pulls the
dielectric into the capacitor.
Fringing Ô¨Āelds are notoriously difÔ¨Ācult to calculate; luckily, we can avoid this
altogether, by the following ingenious method.20 Let W be the energy of the

5.1.1 Magnetic Fields\nRemember the basi.text:
: We have a collection
of charges q1, q2, q3, . . . (the ‚Äúsource‚ÄĚ charges), and we want to calculate the force
they exert on some other charge Q (the ‚Äútest‚ÄĚ charge). (See Fig. 5.1.) According
to the principle of superposition, it is sufÔ¨Ācient to Ô¨Ānd the force of a single source
charge‚ÄĒthe total is then the vector sum of all the individual forces. Up to now,
we have conÔ¨Āned our attention to the simplest case, electrostatics, in which the
source charge is at rest (though the test charge need not be). The time has come
to consider the forces between charges in motion.
To give you some sense of what is in store, imagine that I set up the following
demonstration: Two wires hang from the ceiling, a few centimeters apart; when
I turn on a current, so that it passes up one wire and back down the other, the
wires jump apart‚ÄĒthey evidently repel one another (Fig. 5.2(a)). How do we
explain this? You might suppose that the battery (or whatever drives the current)
is actually charging up the wire, and that the force is simply due to the electrical
repulsion of like charges. But this is incorrect. I could hold up a test charge near
these wires, and there would be no force on it,1 for the wires are in fact electrically
neutral. (It‚Äôs true that electrons are Ô¨āowing down the line‚ÄĒthat‚Äôs what a current
is‚ÄĒbut there are just as many stationary plus charges as moving minus charges
on any given segment.) Moreover, if I hook up my demonstration so as to make
the current Ô¨āow up both wires (Fig. 5.2(b)), they are found to attract!

. It is our Ô¨Ārst encounter with a
magnetic force. Whereas a stationary charge produces only an electric Ô¨Āeld E in
the space around it, a moving charge generates, in addition, a magnetic Ô¨Āeld B.
In fact, magnetic Ô¨Āelds are a lot easier to detect, in practice‚ÄĒall you need is a
Boy Scout compass. How these devices work is irrelevant at the moment; it is
enough to know that the needle points in the direction of the local magnetic Ô¨Āeld.
Ordinarily, this means north, in response to the earth‚Äôs magnetic Ô¨Āeld, but in the
laboratory, where typical Ô¨Āelds may be hundreds of times stronger than that, the
compass indicates the direction of whatever magnetic Ô¨Āeld is present.

Fmag = Q(v √ó B).

This is known as the Lorentz force law.3 In the presence of both electric and
magnetic Ô¨Āelds, the net force on Q would be
F = Q[E + (v √ó B)].

I do not pretend to have derived Eq. 5.1, of course; it is a fundamental axiom

described in Sect. 5.1.1.
Our main job from now on is to calculate the magnetic Ô¨Āeld B (and for that
matter the electric Ô¨Āeld E as well; the rules are more complicated when the source
charges are in motion). But before we proceed, it is worthwhile to take a closer
look at the Lorentz force law itself; it is a peculiar law, and it leads to some truly
bizarre particle trajectories.
Example 5.1. Cyclotron motion.
The archtypical motion of a charged particle

charge Q moves counterclockwise, with speed v, around a circle of radius R,
the magnetic force points inward, and has a Ô¨Āxed magnitude QvB‚ÄĒjust right to
sustain uniform circular motion:
QvB = m v2

5.1.3 Currents\nThe current in a wire is.text:
. By def-
inition, negative charges moving to the left count the same as positive ones to
the right. This conveniently reÔ¨āects the physical fact that almost all phenomena
involving moving charges depend on the product of charge and velocity‚ÄĒif you
reverse the signs of q and v, you get the same answer, so it doesn‚Äôt really mat-
ter which you have. (The Lorentz force law is a case in point; the Hall effect
(Prob. 5.41) is a notorious exception.) In practice, it is ordinarily the negatively
charged electrons that do the moving‚ÄĒin the direction opposite to the electric
current. To avoid the petty complications this entails, I shall often pretend it‚Äôs the
positive charges that move, as in fact everyone assumed they did for a century
or so after Benjamin Franklin established his unfortunate convention.5 Current is
measured in coulombs-per-second, or amperes (A):
1 A = 1 C/s.
(5.12)
5If we called the electron plus and the proton minus, the problem would never arise. In the context of
Franklin‚Äôs experiments with cat‚Äôs fur and glass rods, the choice was completely arbitrary.

5.10 Solution\nFirst of all.text:
, the current must circulate clockwise, in order for (I √ó B) in the hori-
zontal segment to point upward. The force is
Fmag = I Ba,
where a is the width of the loop. (The magnetic forces on the two vertical seg-
ments cancel.) For Fmag to balance the weight (mg), we must therefore have
I = mg
Ba .
(5.18)
The weight just hangs there, suspended in mid-air!
What happens if we now increase the current? Then the upward magnetic force
exceeds the downward force of gravity, and the loop rises, lifting the weight.
Somebody‚Äôs doing work, and it sure looks as though the magnetic force is re-
sponsible. Indeed, one is tempted to write
Wmag = Fmagh = I Bah,
(5.19)
where h is the distance the loop rises. But we know that magnetic forces never do
work. What‚Äôs going on here?
Well, when the loop starts to rise, the charges in the wire are no longer moving
horizontally‚ÄĒtheir velocity now acquires an upward component u, the speed of
the loop (Fig. 5.11), in addition to the horizontal component w associated with
the current (I = őĽw). The magnetic force, which is always perpendicular to the
velocity, no longer points straight up, but tilts back. It is perpendicular to the net
displacement of the charge (which is in the direction of v), and therefore it does
no work on q. It does have a vertical component (qwB); indeed, the net vertical
force on all the charge (őĽa) in the upper segment of the loop is
Fvert = őĽawB = I Ba
(5.20)
(as before); but now it also has a horizontal component (quB), which opposes
the Ô¨āow of current. Whoever is in charge of maintaining that current, therefore,
must now push those charges along, against the backward component of the mag-
netic force.

. Imagine you‚Äôre sliding a trunk
up a frictionless ramp, by pushing on it horizontally with a mop (Fig. 5.12). The
normal force (N) does no work, because it is perpendicular to the displacement.

a (backward) horizontal component (which you have to overcome by pushing on
the mop). Who is doing the work here? You are, obviously‚ÄĒand yet your force
(which is purely horizontal) is not (at least, not directly) what lifts the box. The

First of all, what is the direction of B? Could it have any x component? No: A
glance at the Biot-Savart law (Eq. 5.42) reveals that B is perpendicular to K.

of the Ô¨Āeld). But the z component of B cannot possibly depend on the direction of
the current in the xy plane. (Think about it!) So B can only have a y component,
and a quick check with your right hand should convince you that it points to the
left above the plane and to the right below it.

These are Maxwell‚Äôs equations for electrostatics. Together with the boundary

if the source charge density ŌĀ is given; they contain essentially the same infor-

These are Maxwell‚Äôs equations for magnetostatics. Again, together with the
boundary condition B ‚Üí0 far from all currents, Maxwell‚Äôs equations determine
the magnetic Ô¨Āeld; they are equivalent to the Biot-Savart law (plus superposition).
Maxwell‚Äôs equations and the force law
F = Q(E + v √ó B)
constitute the most elegant formulation of electrostatics and magnetostatics.
The electric Ô¨Āeld diverges away from a (positive) charge; the magnetic Ô¨Āeld
line curls around a current (Fig. 5.44). Electric Ô¨Āeld lines originate on positive
charges and terminate on negative ones; magnetic Ô¨Āeld lines do not begin or end
anywhere‚ÄĒto do so would require a nonzero divergence. They typically form
closed loops or extend out to inÔ¨Ānity.17 To put it another way, there are no point
sources for B, as there are for E; there exists no magnetic analog to electric
charge. This is the physical content of the statement ‚ąá¬∑ B = 0. Coulomb and
others believed that magnetism was produced by magnetic charges (magnetic
monopoles, as we would now call them), and in some older books you will still
Ô¨Ānd references to a magnetic version of Coulomb‚Äôs law, giving the force of at-
traction or repulsion between them. It was Amp√®re who Ô¨Ārst speculated that all
magnetic effects are attributable to electric charges in motion (currents). As far

example‚ÄĒsymmetry considerations can sometimes take the place of boundary conditions.
17A third possibility turns out to be surprisingly common: they can form chaotic tangles. See
M. Lieberherr, Am. J. Phys. 78, 1117 (2010).

5.53 It is clear from Eq.text:
. 5.86 that the magnetic dipole moment is independent of
the choice of origin. You may remember that the electric dipole moment is in-
dependent of the origin only when the total charge vanishes (Sect. 3.4.3). Since
the magnetic monopole moment is always zero, it is not really surprising that the
magnetic dipole moment is always independent of origin.
Although the dipole term dominates the multipole expansion (unless m = 0)
and thus offers a good approximation to the true potential, it is not ordinarily the
exact potential; there will be quadrupole, octopole, and higher contributions. You
might ask, is it possible to devise a current distribution whose potential is ‚Äúpure‚ÄĚ
dipole‚ÄĒfor which Eq. 5.85 is exact? Well, yes and no: like the electrical analog,
it can be done, but the model is a bit contrived. To begin with, you must take an
inÔ¨Ānitesimally small loop at the origin, but then, in order to keep the dipole mo-
ment Ô¨Ānite, you have to crank the current up to inÔ¨Ānity, with the product m = Ia
held Ô¨Āxed. In practice, the dipole potential is a suitable approximation whenever
the distance r greatly exceeds the size of the loop.
The magnetic Ô¨Āeld of a (perfect) dipole is easiest to calculate if we put m at
the origin and let it point in the z-direction (Fig. 5.54). According to Eq. 5.85, the
potential at point (r, őł, ŌÜ) is

6.1.1 Diamagnets.text:
, Paramagnets, Ferromagnets
If you ask the average person what ‚Äúmagnetism‚ÄĚ is, you will probably be told
about refrigerator decorations, compass needles, and the North Pole‚ÄĒnone of
which has any obvious connection with moving charges or current-carrying wires.
Yet all magnetic phenomena are due to electric charges in motion, and in fact, if
you could examine a piece of magnetic material on an atomic scale you would
Ô¨Ānd tiny currents: electrons orbiting around nuclei and spinning about their axes.
For macroscopic purposes, these current loops are so small that we may treat them
as magnetic dipoles. Ordinarily, they cancel each other out because of the random
orientation of the atoms. But when a magnetic Ô¨Āeld is applied, a net alignment of
these magnetic dipoles occurs, and the medium becomes magnetically polarized,
or magnetized.
Unlike electric polarization, which is almost always in the same direction as E,
some materials acquire a magnetization parallel to B (paramagnets) and some
opposite to B (diamagnets). A few substances (called ferromagnets, in defer-
ence to the most common example, iron) retain their magnetization even after the
external Ô¨Āeld has been removed‚ÄĒfor these, the magnetization is not determined
by the present Ô¨Āeld but by the whole magnetic ‚Äúhistory‚ÄĚ of the object. Permanent
magnets made of iron are the most familiar examples of magnetism, but from a
theoretical point of view they are the most complicated; I‚Äôll save ferromagnetism
for the end of the chapter, and begin with qualitative models of paramagnetism
and diamagnetism.

6.1.4 Magnetization\nIn the presence of.text:
Ô¨Āeld, matter becomes magnetized; that is, upon
microscopic examination, it will be found to contain many tiny dipoles, with a net
alignment along some direction. We have discussed two mechanisms that account
for this magnetic polarization: (1) paramagnetism (the dipoles associated with the
spins of unpaired electrons experience a torque tending to line them up parallel
to the Ô¨Āeld) and (2) diamagnetism (the orbital speed of the electrons is altered in
such a way as to change the orbital dipole moment in a direction opposite to the
Ô¨Āeld). Whatever the cause, we describe the state of magnetic polarization by the
vector quantity
M ‚Č°magnetic dipole moment per unit volume.
(6.9)
M is called the magnetization; it plays a role analogous to the polarization
P in electrostatics. In the following section, we will not worry about how the
magnetization got there‚ÄĒit could be paramagnetism, diamagnetism, or even
ferromagnetism‚ÄĒwe shall take M as given, and calculate the Ô¨Āeld this magneti-
zation itself produces.
Incidentally, it may have surprised you to learn that materials other than the
famous ferromagnetic trio (iron, nickel, and cobalt) are affected by a magnetic
Ô¨Āeld at all. You cannot, of course, pick up a piece of wood or aluminum with a
magnet. The reason is that diamagnetism and paramagnetism are extremely weak:
It takes a delicate experiment and a powerful magnet to detect them at all. If you
were to suspend a piece of paramagnetic material above a solenoid, as in Fig. 6.3,
the induced magnetization would be upward, and hence the force downward. By
contrast, the magnetization of a diamagnetic object would be downward and the
force upward. In general, when a sample is placed in a region of nonuniform Ô¨Āeld,
the paramagnet is attracted into the Ô¨Āeld, whereas the diamagnet is repelled away.
But the actual forces are pitifully weak‚ÄĒin a typical experimental arrangement
the force on a comparable sample of iron would be 104 or 105 times as great.
That‚Äôs why it was reasonable for us to calculate the Ô¨Āeld inside a piece of copper
wire, say, in Chapter 5, without worrying about the effects of magnetization.5

6.2.3 The Magnetic Field Inside Matter\n.text:
Ô¨Āeld, the actual microscopic magnetic Ô¨Āeld inside matter Ô¨āuc-
tuates wildly from point to point and instant to instant. When we speak of ‚Äúthe‚ÄĚ
magnetic Ô¨Āeld in matter, we mean the macroscopic Ô¨Āeld: the average over regions
large enough to contain many atoms. (The magnetization M is ‚Äúsmoothed out‚ÄĚ in
the same sense.) It is this macroscopic Ô¨Āeld that one obtains when the methods of
Sect. 6.2.1 are applied to points inside magnetized material, as you can prove for
yourself in the following problem.
Problem 6.11 In Sect, 6.2.1, we began with the potential of a perfect dipole
(Eq. 6.10), whereas in fact we are dealing with physical dipoles. Show, by the
method of Sect. 4.2.3, that we nonetheless get the correct macroscopic Ô¨Āeld.

6.3 THE AUXILIARY FIELD H.text:
6.3.1
Amp√®re‚Äôs Law in Magnetized Materials
In Sect. 6.2, we found that the effect of magnetization is to establish bound cur-
rents Jb = ‚ąá√ó M within the material and Kb = M √ó ňÜn on the surface. The Ô¨Āeld
due to magnetization of the medium is just the Ô¨Āeld produced by these bound cur-
rents. We are now ready to put everything together: the Ô¨Āeld attributable to bound
currents, plus the Ô¨Āeld due to everything else‚ÄĒwhich I shall call the free current.
The free current might Ô¨āow through wires imbedded in the magnetized substance
or, if the latter is a conductor, through the material itself. In any event, the total
current can be written as
J = Jb + J f .
(6.17)
There is no new physics in Eq. 6.17; it is simply a convenience to separate the
current into these two parts, because they got there by quite different means: the

6.19 looks just like Amp√®re‚Äôs original law (Eq. 5.56), except that the
total current is replaced by the free current, and B is replaced by őľ0H. As in the

spondence. It does not say that őľ0H is ‚Äújust like B, only its source is J f instead of
J.‚ÄĚ For the curl alone does not determine a vector Ô¨Āeld‚ÄĒyou must also know the
divergence. And whereas ‚ąá¬∑ B = 0, the divergence of H is not, in general, zero.
In fact, from Eq. 6.18
‚ąá¬∑ H = ‚ąí‚ąá¬∑ M.
(6.23)
Only when the divergence of M vanishes is the parallel between B and őľ0H
faithful.
If you think I‚Äôm being pedantic, consider the example of the bar magnet‚ÄĒa
short cylinder of iron that carries a permanent uniform magnetization M parallel
to its axis. (See Probs. 6.9 and 6.14.) In this case there is no free current any-
where, and a na√Įve application of Eq. 6.20 might lead you to suppose that H = 0,
and hence that B = őľ0M inside the magnet and B = 0 outside, which is non-
sense. It is quite true that the curl of H vanishes everywhere, but the divergence
does not. (Can you see where ‚ąá¬∑ M Őł= 0?) Advice: When you are asked to Ô¨Ānd

problem exhibits cylindrical, plane, solenoidal, or toroidal symmetry, then you
can get H directly from Eq. 6.20 by the usual Amp√®re‚Äôs law methods. (Evidently,
in such cases ‚ąá¬∑ M is automatically zero, since the free current alone determines
the answer.) If the requisite symmetry is absent, you‚Äôll have to think of another

6.4.2 Ferromagnetism\nIn a linear medium.text:
, the alignment of atomic dipoles is maintained by a magnetic
Ô¨Āeld imposed from the outside. Ferromagnets‚ÄĒwhich are emphatically not lin-
ear11‚ÄĒrequire no external Ô¨Āelds to sustain the magnetization; the alignment is
‚Äúfrozen in.‚ÄĚ Like paramagnetism, ferromagnetism involves the magnetic dipoles
associated with the spins of unpaired electrons. The new feature, which makes fer-
romagnetism so different from paramagnetism, is the interaction between nearby
dipoles: In a ferromagnet, each dipole ‚Äúlikes‚ÄĚ to point in the same direction as its
neighbors. The reason for this preference is essentially quantum mechanical, and
I shall not endeavor to explain it here; it is enough to know that the correlation is
so strong as to align virtually 100% of the unpaired electron spins. If you could
somehow magnify a piece of iron and ‚Äúsee‚ÄĚ the individual dipoles as tiny arrows,
it would look something like Fig. 6.25, with all the spins pointing the same way.
But if that is true, why isn‚Äôt every wrench and nail a powerful magnet? The
answer is that the alignment occurs in relatively small patches, called domains.
Each domain contains billions of dipoles, all lined up (these domains are actually
visible under a microscope, using suitable etching techniques‚ÄĒsee Fig. 6.26), but
the domains themselves are randomly oriented. The household wrench contains an
enormous number of domains, and their magnetic Ô¨Āelds cancel, so the wrench as
a whole is not magnetized. (Actually, the orientation of domains is not completely
random; within a given crystal, there may be some preferential alignment along
the crystal axes. But there will be just as many domains pointing one way as
the other, so there is still no large-scale magnetization. Moreover, the crystals
themselves are randomly oriented within any sizable chunk of metal.)
How, then, would you produce a permanent magnet, such as they sell in
toy stores? If you put a piece of iron into a strong magnetic Ô¨Āeld, the torque
N = m √ó B tends to align the dipoles parallel to the Ô¨Āeld. Since they like to stay
parallel to their neighbors, most of the dipoles will resist this torque. However,
FIGURE 6.25
11In this sense, it is misleading to speak of the susceptibility or permeability of a ferromagnet. The
terms are used for such materials, but they refer to the proportionality factor between a differential
increase in H and the resulting differential change in M (or B); moreover, they are not constants, but
functions of H.

6.27 M drops down to zero.text:
(point d). If you turn I still higher, you soon reach sat-
uration in the other direction‚ÄĒall the dipoles now pointing to the right (e). At
this stage, switching off the current will leave the wrench with a permanent mag-
netization to the right (point f ). To complete the story, turn I on again in the
positive sense: M returns to zero (point g), and eventually to the forward satura-
tion point (b).
The path we have traced out is called a hysteresis loop. Notice that the mag-
netization of the wrench depends not only on the applied Ô¨Āeld (that is, on I), but
also on its previous magnetic ‚Äúhistory.‚ÄĚ12 For instance, at three different times in
our experiment the current was zero (a, c, and f ), yet the magnetization was dif-
ferent for each of them. Actually, it is customary to draw hysteresis loops as plots
of B against H, rather than M against I. (If our coil is approximated by a long
solenoid, with n turns per unit length, then H = nI, so H and I are proportional.

7.1 ELECTROMOTIVE FORCE.text:
7.1.1
Ohm‚Äôs Law
To make a current Ô¨āow, you have to push on the charges. How fast they move,
in response to a given push, depends on the nature of the material. For most sub-
stances, the current density J is proportional to the force per unit charge, f:
J = ŌÉf.
(7.1)
The proportionality factor ŌÉ (not to be confused with surface charge) is an empir-
ical constant that varies from one material to another; it‚Äôs called the conductivity
of the medium. Actually, the handbooks usually list the reciprocal of ŌÉ, called
the resistivity: ŌĀ = 1/ŌÉ (not to be confused with charge density‚ÄĒI‚Äôm sorry, but
we‚Äôre running out of Greek letters, and this is the standard notation). Some typical
values are listed in Table 7.1. Notice that even insulators conduct slightly, though
the conductivity of a metal is astronomically greater; in fact, for most purposes
metals can be regarded as perfect conductors, with ŌÉ = ‚ąě, while for insulators
we can pretend ŌÉ = 0.
In principle, the force that drives the charges to produce the current could be
anything‚ÄĒchemical, gravitational, or trained ants with tiny harnesses. For our
purposes, though, it‚Äôs usually an electromagnetic force that does the job. In this
case Eq. 7.1 becomes
J = ŌÉ(E + v √ó B).
(7.2)
Ordinarily, the velocity of the charges is sufÔ¨Āciently small that the second term
can be ignored:
J = ŌÉE.
(7.3)
(However, in plasmas, for instance, the magnetic contribution to f can be signif-
icant.) Equation 7.3 is called Ohm‚Äôs law, though the physics behind it is really
contained in Eq. 7.1, of which 7.3 is just a special case.
I know: you‚Äôre confused because I said E = 0 inside a conductor (Sect. 2.5.1).

7.1.2 Electromotive Force\nIf you think.text:
‚ÄĒa battery hooked up to a light bulb,
say (Fig. 7.7)‚ÄĒa perplexing question arises: In practice, the current is the same all
the way around the loop; why is this the case, when the only obvious driving force
is inside the battery? Off hand, you might expect a large current in the battery and
none at all in the lamp. Who‚Äôs doing the pushing, in the rest of the circuit, and how
does it happen that this push is exactly right to produce the same current in each
segment? What‚Äôs more, given that the charges in a typical wire move (literally)
at a snail‚Äôs pace (see Prob. 5.20), why doesn‚Äôt it take half an hour for the current
to reach the light bulb? How do all the charges know to start moving at the same
instant?
Answer: If the current were not the same all the way around (for instance, dur-
ing the Ô¨Ārst split second after the switch is closed), then charge would be piling up
somewhere, and‚ÄĒhere‚Äôs the crucial point‚ÄĒthe electric Ô¨Āeld of this accumulating
charge is in such a direction as to even out the Ô¨āow. Suppose, for instance, that
the current into the bend in Fig. 7.8 is greater than the current out. Then charge
piles up at the ‚Äúknee,‚ÄĚ and this produces a Ô¨Āeld aiming away from the kink.3 This
Ô¨Āeld opposes the current Ô¨āowing in (slowing it down) and promotes the current
Ô¨āowing out (speeding it up) until these currents are equal, at which point there is
no further accumulation of charge, and equilibrium is established. It‚Äôs a beautiful
system, automatically self-correcting to keep the current uniform, and it does it
all so quickly that, in practice, you can safely assume the current is the same all
around the circuit, even in systems that oscillate at radio frequencies.

In 1831 Michael Faraday reported on a series of experiments, including three that
(with some violence to history) can be characterized as follows:
Experiment 1. He pulled a loop of wire to the right through a magnetic Ô¨Āeld
(Fig. 7.21a). A current Ô¨āowed in the loop.
Experiment 2. He moved the magnet to the left, holding the loop still (Fig. 7.21b).
Again, a current Ô¨āowed in the loop.
Experiment 3. With both the loop and the magnet at rest (Fig. 7.21c), he changed
the strength of the Ô¨Āeld (he used an electromagnet, and varied the current
in the coil). Once again, current Ô¨āowed in the loop.

7.22 Solution\nThe magnetic.text:

7.23 Keeping track of the signs in Farad.text:
‚Äôs law can be a real headache. For in-
stance, in Ex. 7.5 we would like to know which way around the ring the induced
current Ô¨āows. In principle, the right-hand rule does the job (we called  positive
to the left, in Fig. 7.22, so the positive direction for current in the ring is counter-
clockwise, as viewed from the left; since the Ô¨Ārst spike in Fig. 7.23b is negative,
the Ô¨Ārst current pulse Ô¨āows clockwise, and the second counterclockwise). But
there‚Äôs a handy rule, called Lenz‚Äôs law, whose sole purpose is to help you get the
directions right:10
Nature abhors a change in Ô¨āux.
The induced current will Ô¨āow in such a direction that the Ô¨āux it produces tends
to cancel the change. (As the front end of the magnet in Ex. 7.5 enters the ring,
the Ô¨āux increases, so the current in the ring must generate a Ô¨Āeld to the right‚ÄĒit
therefore Ô¨āows clockwise.) Notice that it is the change in Ô¨āux, not the Ô¨āux it-
self, that nature abhors (when the tail end of the magnet exits the ring, the Ô¨āux
drops, so the induced current Ô¨āows counterclockwise, in an effort to restore it).
Faraday induction is a kind of ‚Äúinertial‚ÄĚ phenomenon: A conducting loop ‚Äúlikes‚ÄĚ
to maintain a constant Ô¨āux through it; if you try to change the Ô¨āux, the loop re-
sponds by sending a current around in such a direction as to frustrate your efforts.
(It doesn‚Äôt succeed completely; the Ô¨āux produced by the induced current is typi-
cally only a tiny fraction of the original. All Lenz‚Äôs law tells you is the direction of
the Ô¨āow.)
10Lenz‚Äôs law applies to motional emfs, too, but for them it is usually easier to get the direction of the
current from the Lorentz force law.

7.24 Solution\nBefore you turned on the.text:
, the Ô¨āux through the ring was zero. Afterward a
Ô¨āux appeared (upward, in the diagram), and the emf generated in the ring led to a
current (in the ring) which, according to Lenz‚Äôs law, was in such a direction that
its Ô¨Āeld tended to cancel this new Ô¨āux. This means that the current in the loop is
opposite to the current in the solenoid. And opposite currents repel, so the ring
Ô¨āies off.11

Inductance (like capacitance) is an intrinsically positive quantity. Lenz‚Äôs law,
which is enforced by the minus sign in Eq. 7.27, dictates that the emf is in such
a direction as to oppose any change in current. For this reason, it is called a
back emf. Whenever you try to alter the current in a wire, you must Ô¨Āght against
this back emf. Inductance plays somewhat the same role in electric circuits that
mass plays in mechanical systems: The greater L is, the harder it is to change
the current, just as the larger the mass, the harder it is to change an object‚Äôs
velocity.
Example 7.12.
Suppose a current I is Ô¨āowing around a loop, when someone
suddenly cuts the wire. The current drops ‚Äúinstantaneously‚ÄĚ to zero. This gen-
erates a whopping back emf, for although I may be small, dI/dt is enormous.
(That‚Äôs why you sometimes draw a spark when you unplug an iron or toaster‚ÄĒ
electromagnetic induction is desperately trying to keep the current going, even if
it has to jump the gap in the circuit.)
Nothing so dramatic occurs when you plug in a toaster or iron. In this case in-
duction opposes the sudden increase in current, prescribing instead a smooth and

talking about the energy delivered to the resistors and converted into heat‚ÄĒthat
is irretrievably lost, as far as the circuit is concerned, and can be large or small,
depending on how long you let the current run. What I am concerned with, rather,
is the work you must do against the back emf to get the current going. This is
a Ô¨Āxed amount, and it is recoverable: you get it back when the current is turned
off. In the meantime, it represents energy latent in the circuit; as we‚Äôll see in a
moment, it can be regarded as energy stored in the magnetic Ô¨Āeld.

7.45 Problem.text:
7.35 The preceding problem was an artiÔ¨Ācial model for the charging ca-
pacitor, designed to avoid complications associated with the current spreading out
over the surface of the plates. For a more realistic model, imagine thin wires that
connect to the centers of the plates (Fig. 7.46a). Again, the current I is constant,
the radius of the capacitor is a, and the separation of the plates is w ‚Č™a. Assume
that the current Ô¨āows out over the plates in such a way that the surface charge is
uniform, at any given time, and is zero at t = 0.
(a) Find the electric Ô¨Āeld between the plates, as a function of t.
(b) Find the displacement current through a circle of radius s in the plane mid-
way between the plates. Using this circle as your ‚ÄúAmperian loop,‚ÄĚ and the Ô¨āat
surface that spans it, Ô¨Ānd the magnetic Ô¨Āeld at a distance s from the axis.

7.3.5 Maxwell.text:
‚Äôs Equations in Matter
Maxwell‚Äôs equations in the form 7.40 are complete and correct as they stand.
However, when you are working with materials that are subject to electric and
magnetic polarization there is a more convenient way to write them. For inside
polarized matter there will be accumulations of ‚Äúbound‚ÄĚ charge and current, over
which you exert no direct control. It would be nice to reformulate Maxwell‚Äôs
equations so as to make explicit reference only to the ‚Äúfree‚ÄĚ charges and currents.
We have already learned, from the static case, that an electric polarization P
produces a bound charge density
ŌĀb = ‚ąí‚ąá¬∑ P
(7.47)
(Eq. 4.12). Likewise, a magnetic polarization (or ‚Äúmagnetization‚ÄĚ) M results in a
bound current
Jb = ‚ąá√ó M
(7.48)
(Eq. 6.13). There‚Äôs just one new feature to consider in the nonstatic case: Any
change in the electric polarization involves a Ô¨āow of (bound) charge (call it Jp),
which must be included in the total current. For suppose we examine a tiny chunk
of polarized material (Fig. 7.47). The polarization introduces a charge density
ŌÉb = P at one end and ‚ąíŌÉb at the other (Eq. 4.11). If P now increases a bit, the
charge on each end increases accordingly, giving a net current
dI = ‚ąāŌÉb

This polarization current has nothing to do with the bound current Jb. The
latter is associated with magnetization of the material and involves the spin and
orbital motion of electrons; Jp, by contrast, is the result of the linear motion of
charge when the electric polarization changes. If P points to the right, and is
increasing, then each plus charge moves a bit to the right and each minus charge
to the left; the cumulative effect is the polarization current Jp. We ought to check
that Eq. 7.49 is consistent with the continuity equation:

Problem 7.58 A transformer (Prob. 7.57) takes an input AC voltage of amplitude
V1, and delivers an output voltage of amplitude V2, which is determined by the turns
ratio (V2/V1 = N2/N1). If N2 > N1, the output voltage is greater than the input
voltage. Why doesn‚Äôt this violate conservation of energy? Answer: Power is the
product of voltage and current; if the voltage goes up, the current must come down.
The purpose of this problem is to see exactly how this works out, in a simpliÔ¨Āed
model.

tum, in electrodynamics. But I want to begin by reviewing the conservation of
charge, because it is the paradigm for all conservation laws. What precisely does
conservation of charge tell us? That the total charge in the universe is constant?
Well, sure‚ÄĒthat‚Äôs global conservation of charge. But local conservation of charge
is a much stronger statement: If the charge in some region changes, then exactly
that amount of charge must have passed in or out through the surface. The tiger
can‚Äôt simply rematerialize outside the cage; if it got from inside to outside it must
have slipped through a hole in the fence.

This is the ‚Äúcontinuity equation‚ÄĚ for energy‚ÄĒu (energy density) plays the role of
ŌĀ (charge density), and S takes the part of J (current density). It expresses local
conservation of electromagnetic energy.
In general, though, electromagnetic energy by itself is not conserved (nor is
the energy of the charges). Of course not! The Ô¨Āelds do work on the charges, and
the charges create Ô¨Āelds‚ÄĒenergy is tossed back and forth between them. In the
overall energy economy, you must include the contributions of both the matter
and the Ô¨Āelds.

8.2.1 Newton.text:
‚Äôs Third Law in Electrodynamics
Imagine a point charge q traveling in along the x axis at a constant speed v.
Because it is moving, its electric Ô¨Āeld is not given by Coulomb‚Äôs law; never-
theless, E still points radially outward from the instantaneous position of the
charge (Fig. 8.2a), as we‚Äôll see in Chapter 10. Since, moreover, a moving point
charge does not constitute a steady current, its magnetic Ô¨Āeld is not given by the
Biot-Savart law. Nevertheless, it‚Äôs a fact that B still circles around the axis in a
manner suggested by the right-hand rule (Fig. 8.2b); again, the proof will come in
Chapter 10.
4What about energy Ô¨āow down the wire? For a discussion, see M. K. Harbola, Am. J. Phys. 78, 1203
(2010). For a more sophisticated geometry, see B. S. Davis and L. Kaplan, Am. J. Phys. 79, 1155
(2011).

8.2 Now suppose this charge encounters a.text:
, proceeding in at the same
speed along the y axis. Of course, the electromagnetic force between them would
tend to drive them off the axes, but let‚Äôs assume that they‚Äôre mounted on tracks,
or something, so they‚Äôre obliged to maintain the same direction and the same
speed (Fig. 8.3). The electric force between them is repulsive, but how about the
magnetic force? Well, the magnetic Ô¨Āeld of q1 points into the page (at the position
of q2), so the magnetic force on q2 is toward the right, whereas the magnetic
Ô¨Āeld of q2 is out of the page (at the position of q1), and the magnetic force on
q1 is upward. The net electromagnetic force of q1 on q2 is equal but not opposite
to the force of q2 on q1, in violation of Newton‚Äôs third law. In electrostatics and
magnetostatics the third law holds, but in electrodynamics it does not.
Well, that‚Äôs an interesting curiosity, but then, how often does one actually use
the third law, in practice? Answer: All the time! For the proof of conservation of
momentum rests on the cancellation of internal forces, which follows from the
third law. When you tamper with the third law, you are placing conservation of
momentum in jeopardy, and there is hardly any principle in physics more sacred
than that.
Momentum conservation is rescued, in electrodynamics, by the realization
that the Ô¨Āelds themselves carry momentum. This is not so surprising when you

9.1.1 The Wave Equation\nWhat is a.text:
‚Äúwave‚ÄĚ? I don‚Äôt think I can give you an entirely satisfactory answer‚ÄĒthe
concept is intrinsically somewhat vague‚ÄĒbut here‚Äôs a start: A wave is a distur-
bance of a continuous medium that propagates with a Ô¨Āxed shape at constant ve-
locity. Immediately I must add qualiÔ¨Āers: In the presence of absorption, the wave
will diminish in size as it moves; if the medium is dispersive, different frequencies
travel at different speeds; in two or three dimensions, as the wave spreads out, its
amplitude will decrease; and of course standing waves don‚Äôt propagate at all. But
these are reÔ¨Ānements; let‚Äôs start with the simple case: Ô¨Āxed shape, constant speed
(Fig. 9.1).
How would you represent such an object mathematically? In the Ô¨Āgure, I have
drawn the wave at two different times, once at t = 0, and again at some later
time t‚ÄĒeach point on the wave form simply shifts to the right by an amount vt,
where v is the velocity. Maybe the wave is generated by shaking one end of a taut
string; f (z, t) represents the displacement of the string at the point z, at time t.
Given the initial shape of the string, g(z) ‚Č°f (z, 0), what is the subsequent form,
f (z, t)? Well, the displacement at point z, at the later time t, is the same as the
displacement a distance vt to the left (i.e. at z ‚ąívt), back at time t = 0:
f (z, t) = f (z ‚ąívt, 0) = g(z ‚ąívt).
(9.1)
That statement captures (mathematically) the essence of wave motion. It tells us
that the function f (z, t), which might have depended on z and t in any old way,
in fact depends on them only in the very special combination z ‚ąívt; when that
