. 9.3 I stipulated that the free charge density ŌĀ f and the free current density
J f are zero, and everything that followed was predicated on that assumption. Such
a restriction is perfectly reasonable when you‚Äôre talking about wave propagation
through a vacuum or through insulating materials such as glass or (pure) water.
But in the case of conductors we do not independently control the Ô¨āow of charge,
and in general J f is certainly not zero. In fact, according to Ohm‚Äôs law, the (free)

Thus any initial free charge ŌĀ f (0) dissipates in a characteristic time ŌĄ ‚Č°ŌĶ/ŌÉ. This
reÔ¨āects the familiar fact that if you put some free charge on a conductor, it will
Ô¨āow out to the edges. The time constant ŌĄ affords a measure of how ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ a con-
ductor is: For a ‚Äúperfect‚ÄĚ conductor, ŌÉ = ‚ąěand ŌĄ = 0; for a ‚Äúgood‚ÄĚ conductor,
ŌĄ is much less than the other relevant times in the problem (in oscillatory systems,
that means ŌĄ ‚Č™1/ŌČ); for a ‚Äúpoor‚ÄĚ conductor, ŌĄ is greater than the characteris-
tic times in the problem (ŌĄ ‚Čę1/ŌČ).17 But we‚Äôre not interested in this transient
17 N. Ashby, Am. J. Phys. 43, 553 (1975), points out that for good conductors ŌĄ is absurdly short
(10‚ąí19 s, for copper, whereas the time between collisions is ŌĄc = 10‚ąí14 s). The problem is that Ohm‚Äôs
law itself breaks down on time scales shorter than ŌĄc; actually, the time it takes free charge to dissipate
in a good conductor is of order ŌĄc, not ŌĄ. Moreover, H. C. Ohanian, Am. J. Phys. 51, 1020 (1983),
shows that it takes even longer for the Ô¨Āelds and currents to equilibrate. But none of this is relevant
to our present purpose; the net free charge density in a conductor does quickly dissipate, and exactly
how long the process takes is beside the point.

9.4.3 The Frequency Dependence of Permit.text:
, we have seen that the propagation of electromagnetic
waves through matter is governed by three properties of the material: the permit-
tivity ŌĶ, the permeability őľ, and the conductivity ŌÉ. Actually, each of these param-
eters depends to some extent on the frequency of the waves you are considering.
Indeed, it is well known from optics that n ‚ąľ= ‚ąöŌĶr is a function of wavelength
(Fig. 9.19 shows the graph for a typical glass). A prism or a raindrop bends blue
light more sharply than red, and spreads white light out into a rainbow of colors.
This phenomenon is called dispersion. By extension, whenever the speed of a
wave depends on its frequency, the supporting medium is called dispersive.

[You can demonstrate this by dropping a rock into the nearest pond and watching
the waves that form: While the disturbance as a whole spreads out in a circle,
moving at speed vg, the ripples that go to make it up will be seen to travel twice
as fast (v = 2vg in this case). They appear at the back end of the packet, growing
as they move forward to the center, then shrinking again and fading away at the
front (Fig. 9.20).] We shall not concern ourselves with these matters‚ÄĒI‚Äôll stick

just mention that the energy carried by a wave packet in a dispersive medium
does not travel at the phase velocity. Don‚Äôt be too alarmed, therefore, if in some
circumstances v comes out greater than c.19

The actual binding forces can be quite complicated, but we shall picture each electron as attached to the end of a spring, with force constant kspring (Fig. 9.21): Fbinding = ‚ąíkspringx = ‚ąímŌČ2 0x, (9.151) where x is displacement from equilibrium, m is the electron‚Äôs mass, and ŌČ0 is the natural oscillation frequency, kspring/m. [If this strikes you as an implausible model, look back at Ex. 4.1, where we were led to a force of precisely this form. As a matter of fact, practically any binding force can be approximated this way for sufÔ¨Āciently small displacements from equilibrium, as you can see by expanding the potential energy in a Taylor series about the equilibrium point: U(x) = U(0) + xU ‚Ä≤(0) + 1 2x2U ‚Ä≤‚Ä≤(0) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ . The Ô¨Ārst term is a constant, with no dynamical signiÔ¨Ācance (you can always adjust the zero of potential energy so that U (0) = 0). The second term automatically vanishes, since dU/dx = ‚ąíF, and by the nature of an equilibrium, the force at that point is zero. The third term is precisely the potential energy of a spring with force constant kspring = d2U/dx2 0 (the second derivative is positive, for a point of stable equilibrium). As long as the displacements are small, the higher terms in the series can be neglected. (10.20) This is reminiscent of the standard formula from mechanics, for the motion of a particle whose potential energy U is a speciÔ¨Āed function of position: dp dt = ‚ąí‚ąáU. Playing the role of p is the so-called canonical momentum, pcan = p + qA, (10.21) while the part of U is taken by the velocity-dependent quantity Uvel = q(V ‚ąív ¬∑ A). (10.22)

10.1.2 Gauge Transformations\nEquations.text:
10.4 and 10.5 are ugly, and you might be inclined to abandon the
potential formulation altogether. However, we have succeeded in reducing six
problems‚ÄĒÔ¨Ānding E and B (three components each)‚ÄĒdown to four: V (one com-
ponent) and A (three more). Moreover, Eqs. 10.2 and 10.3 do not uniquely deÔ¨Āne
the potentials; we are free to impose extra conditions on V and A, as long as
nothing happens to E and B. Let‚Äôs work out precisely what this gauge freedom
entails.
Suppose we have two sets of potentials, (V, A) and (V ‚Ä≤, A‚Ä≤), which correspond
to the same electric and magnetic Ô¨Āelds. By how much can they differ? Write
A‚Ä≤ = A + őĪ
and V ‚Ä≤ = V + ő≤.
Since the two A‚Äôs give the same B, their curls must be equal, and hence
‚ąá√ó őĪ = 0.
We can therefore write őĪ as the gradient of some scalar:
őĪ = ‚ąáőĽ.
The two potentials also give the same E, so
‚ąáő≤ + ‚ąāőĪ
‚ąāt = 0,

is not equal to the charge of the particle (and depends, through tr, on the location
of the point r). To calculate the total charge of a conÔ¨Āguration, you must inte-
grate ŌĀ over the entire distribution at one instant of time, but here the retardation,
tr = t ‚ąír/c, obliges us to evaluate ŌĀ at different times for different parts of the
conÔ¨Āguration. If the source is moving, this will give a distorted picture of the total
charge. You might think that this problem would disappear for point charges, but
it doesn‚Äôt. In Maxwell‚Äôs electrodynamics, formulated as it is in terms of charge
and current densities, a point charge must be regarded as the limit of an extended
charge, when the size goes to zero. And for an extended particle, no matter how
small, the retardation in Eq. 10.41 throws in a factor (1 ‚ąíňÜr ¬∑ v/c)‚ąí1, where v is

and 11.17) satisfy the Lorenz gauge condition. Do not use approximation 3.

ordinary radio (say, d = 5 cm), should you worry about the radiative contribution
to the total resistance?

!

Problem 11.34
(a) Does a particle in hyperbolic motion (Eq. 10.52) radiate? (Use the exact formula
(Eq. 11.75) to calculate the power radiated.)
(b) Does a particle in hyperbolic motion experience a radiation reaction? (Use the
exact formula (Prob. 11.33) to determine the reaction force.)
[Comment: These famous questions carry important implications for the prin-
ciple of equivalence.27]

ideal electric dipole, p(t), at the origin. Check that your answer is consistent with
Eq. 11.22, in the case of sinusoidal time dependence, and with Prob. 11.26, in the
case of quadratic time dependence.

11.2.2 Radiation Reaction\nAn accelerati.text:
. This radiation carries off energy, which comes,
ultimately, at the expense of the particle‚Äôs kinetic energy. Under the inÔ¨āuence of
a given force, therefore, a charged particle accelerates less than a neutral one of
the same mass. The radiation exerts a force (Frad) back on the charge‚ÄĒa recoil
force, rather like that of a bullet on a gun. In this section I‚Äôll derive the radiation
reaction force from conservation of energy. Then, in the next section, I‚Äôll show
you the actual mechanism responsible, and derive the reaction force again in the
context of a simple model.
For a nonrelativistic particle (v ‚Č™c), the total power radiated is given by the
Larmor formula (Eq. 11.70):

Conservation of energy suggests that this is also the rate at which the particle loses
energy, under the inÔ¨āuence of the radiation reaction force Frad:
Frad ¬∑ v = ‚ąíőľ0q2a2
6ŌÄc
.
(11.77)
I say ‚Äúsuggests‚ÄĚ advisedly, because this equation is actually wrong. For we cal-
culated the radiated power by integrating the Poynting vector over a sphere of
‚ÄúinÔ¨Ānite‚ÄĚ radius; in this calculation the velocity Ô¨Āelds played no part, since they
fall off too rapidly as a function of r to make any contribution. But the velocity
Ô¨Āelds do carry energy‚ÄĒthey just don‚Äôt transport it out to inÔ¨Ānity. As the parti-
cle accelerates and decelerates, energy is exchanged between it and the velocity
Ô¨Āelds, at the same time as energy is irretrievably radiated away by the acceleration
Ô¨Āelds. Equation 11.77 accounts only for the latter, but if we want to know the re-
coil force exerted by the Ô¨Āelds on the charge, we need to consider the total power
lost at any instant, not just the portion that eventually escapes in the form of ra-
diation. (The term ‚Äúradiation reaction‚ÄĚ is a misnomer. We should really call it the
Ô¨Āeld reaction. In fact, we‚Äôll soon see that Frad is determined by the time deriva-
tive of the acceleration and can be nonzero even when the acceleration itself is
instantaneously zero, and the particle is not radiating.)
The energy lost by the particle in any given time interval, then, must equal
the energy carried away by the radiation plus whatever extra energy has been
pumped into the velocity Ô¨Āelds.10 However, if we agree to consider only intervals

11.2.3 The Mechanism Responsible for the.text:
, I derived the Abraham-Lorentz formula for the radiation re-
action, using conservation of energy. I made no attempt to identify the actual
mechanism responsible for this force, except to point out that it must be a recoil
effect of the particle‚Äôs own Ô¨Āelds acting back on the charge. Unfortunately, the
Ô¨Āelds of a point charge blow up right at the particle, so it‚Äôs hard to see how one
can calculate the force they exert.14 Let‚Äôs avoid this problem by considering an
extended charge distribution, for which the Ô¨Āeld is Ô¨Ānite everywhere; at the end,
we‚Äôll take the limit as the size of the charge goes to zero. In general, the electro-
magnetic force of one part (A) on another part (B) is not equal and opposite to
the force of B on A (Fig. 11.16). If the distribution is divided up into inÔ¨Ānitesimal
chunks, and the imbalances are added up for all such pairs, the result is a net force
of the charge on itself. It is this self-force, resulting from the breakdown of New-
ton‚Äôs third law within the structure of the particle, that accounts for the radiation
reaction.
Lorentz originally calculated the electromagnetic self-force using a spherical
charge distribution, which seems reasonable but makes the mathematics rather
cumbersome.15 Because I am only trying to elucidate the mechanism involved,
I shall use a less realistic model: a ‚Äúdumbbell‚ÄĚ in which the total charge q is
divided into two halves separated by a Ô¨Āxed distance d (Fig. 11.17). This is the
simplest possible arrangement of the charge that permits the essential mechanism

40, 1843 (1972), and references cited there.

12.1.1 Einstein.text:
‚Äôs Postulates
Classical mechanics obeys the principle of relativity: the same laws apply in
any inertial reference frame. By ‚Äúinertial‚ÄĚ I mean that the system is at rest or
moving with constant velocity.1 Imagine, for example, that you have loaded a
billiard table onto a railroad car, and the train is going at constant speed down
a smooth straight track. The game will proceed exactly the same as it would
if the train were parked in the station; you don‚Äôt have to ‚Äúcorrect‚ÄĚ your shots
for the fact that the train is moving‚ÄĒindeed, if you pulled all the curtains, you
would have no way of knowing whether the train was moving or not. Notice
by contrast that you know immediately if the train speeds up, or slows down,
or rounds a corner, or goes over a bump‚ÄĒthe billiard balls roll in weird curved
trajectories, and you yourself feel a lurch and spill coffee on your shirt. The
laws of mechanics, then, are certainly not the same in accelerating reference
frames.
In its application to classical mechanics, the principle of relativity is hardly
new; it was stated clearly by Galileo. Question: does it also apply to the laws of
electrodynamics? At Ô¨Ārst glance, the answer would seem to be no. After all, a
charge in motion produces a magnetic Ô¨Āeld, whereas a charge at rest does not. A
charge carried along by the train would generate a magnetic Ô¨Āeld, but someone on
the train, applying the laws of electrodynamics in that system, would predict no
magnetic Ô¨Āeld. In fact, many of the equations of electrodynamics, starting with
the Lorentz force law, make explicit reference to ‚Äúthe‚ÄĚ velocity of the charge.
It certainly appears, therefore, that electromagnetic theory presupposes the exis-
tence of a unique stationary reference frame, with respect to which all velocities
are to be measured.
And yet there is an extraordinary coincidence that gives us pause. Suppose we
mount a wire loop on a freight car, and have the train pass between the poles of a
1This raises an awkward problem: If the laws of physics hold just as well in a uniformly moving frame,
then we have no way of identifying the ‚Äúrest‚ÄĚ frame in the Ô¨Ārst place, and hence no way of checking
that some other frame is moving at constant velocity. To avoid this trap, we deÔ¨Āne an inertial frame
formally as one in which Newton‚Äôs Ô¨Ārst law holds. If you want to know whether you‚Äôre in an inertial
frame, throw some rocks around‚ÄĒif they travel in straight lines at constant speed, you‚Äôve got yourself
an inertial frame, and any frame moving at constant velocity with respect to you will be another inertial
frame (see Prob. 12.1).

When we were discussing simultaneity, I showed that the time ordering of two
events can, at least in certain cases, be reversed, simply by going into a mov-
ing system. But we now see that this is not always possible: If the displacement
4-vector between two events is timelike, their ordering is absolute; if the interval
is spacelike, their ordering depends on the inertial system from which they are
observed. In terms of the space-time diagram, an event on the upper sheet of a
timelike hyperboloid deÔ¨Ānitely occurred after (0, 0), and one on the lower sheet
certainly occurred before; but an event on a spacelike hyperboloid occurred at
positive t, or negative t, depending on your reference frame. This is not an idle
curiosity, for it rescues the notion of causality, on which all physics is based. If
it were always possible to reverse the order of two events, then we could never
say ‚ÄúA caused B,‚ÄĚ since a rival observer would retort that B preceded A. This
embarrassment is avoided, provided the two events are timelike or lightlike sepa-
rated. And causally related events are‚ÄĒotherwise no inÔ¨āuence could travel from
one to the other. Conclusion: The displacement between causally related events is
always timelike, and their temporal ordering is the same for all inertial observers.

12.2 While the idea of this experiment c.text:
, its execution is another
matter, because light travels so inconveniently fast. If it weren‚Äôt for that ‚Äútechnical
detail,‚ÄĚ you could do it with a Ô¨āashlight and a stopwatch. As it happened, an
elaborate and lovely experiment was devised by Michelson and Morley, using an
optical interferometer of fantastic precision. I shall not go into the details here,
because I do not want to distract your attention from the two essential points: (1)
all Michelson and Morley were trying to do was compare the speed of light in
different directions, and (2) what they in fact discovered was that this speed is
exactly the same in all directions.
Nowadays, when students are taught in high school to snicker at the na√Įvet√© of
the ether model, it takes some imagination to comprehend how utterly perplexing
this result must have been at the time. All other waves (water waves, sound waves,
waves on a string) travel at a prescribed speed relative to the propagating medium
(the stuff that does the waving), and if this medium is in motion with respect to
the observer, the net speed is always greater ‚Äúdownstream‚ÄĚ than ‚Äúupstream.‚ÄĚ Over
the next 20 years, a series of improbable schemes were concocted in an effort
to explain why this does not occur with light. Michelson and Morley themselves
interpreted their experiment as conÔ¨Ārmation of the ‚Äúether drag‚ÄĚ hypothesis, which
held that the earth somehow pulls the ether along with it. But this was found to be
inconsistent with other observations, notably the aberration of starlight.3 Various
so-called ‚Äúemission‚ÄĚ theories were proposed, according to which the speed of
electromagnetic waves is governed by the motion of the source‚ÄĒas it would be in
3A discussion of the Michelson-Morley experiment and related matters is to be found in R. Resnick‚Äôs
Introduction to special relativity (New York: John Wiley, 1968), Chapter 1.

moving clock. If you hold x Ô¨Āxed, then you‚Äôre watching a whole series of differ-

running slow.

to the right at speed v in S). Its rest length (that is, its length as measured in ¬ĮS) is

then,

12.3.1 Magnetism as a Relativistic Pheno.text:
, classical electrodynamics is already consistent
with special relativity. Maxwell‚Äôs equations and the Lorentz force law can be ap-
plied legitimately in any inertial system. Of course, what one observer interprets
as an electrical process another may regard as magnetic, but the actual particle
motions they predict will be identical. To the extent that this did not work out
for Lorentz and others, who studied the question in the late nineteenth century,
the fault lay with the nonrelativistic mechanics they used, not with the electro-
dynamics. Having corrected Newtonian mechanics, we are now in a position to
develop a complete and consistent formulation of relativistic electrodynamics.
I emphasize that we will not be changing the rules of electrodynamics in the
slightest‚ÄĒrather, we will be expressing these rules in a notation that exposes and
illuminates their relativistic character. As we go along, I shall pause now and then
to rederive, using the Lorentz transformations, results obtained earlier by more
laborious means. But the main purpose of this section is to provide you with a
deeper understanding of the structure of electrodynamics‚ÄĒlaws that had seemed
arbitrary and unrelated before take on a kind of coherence and inevitability when
approached from the point of view of relativity.
To begin with, I‚Äôd like to show you why there had to be such a thing as mag-
netism, given electrostatics and relativity, and how, in particular, you can calculate
the magnetic force between a current-carrying wire and a moving charge with-
out ever invoking the laws of magnetism.23 Suppose you had a string of positive
charges moving along to the right at speed v. I‚Äôll assume the charges are close
enough together so that we may treat them as a continuous line charge őĽ. Super-
imposed on this positive string is a negative one, ‚ąíőĽ proceeding to the left at the
same speed v. We have, then, a net current to the right, of magnitude
I = 2őĽv.
(12.76)
Meanwhile, a distance s away there is a point charge q traveling to the right
at speed u < v (Fig. 12.34a). Because the two line charges cancel, there is no
electrical force on q in this system (S).
However, let‚Äôs examine the same situation from the point of view of system ¬ĮS,
which moves to the right with speed u (Fig. 12.34b). In this reference frame, q
is at rest. By the Einstein velocity addition rule, the velocities of the positive and
negative lines are now

12.3.2 How the Fields Transform\nWe have.text:
, in various special cases, that one observer‚Äôs electric Ô¨Āeld is
another‚Äôs magnetic Ô¨Āeld. It would be nice to know the general transformation
rules for electromagnetic Ô¨Āelds: Given the Ô¨Āelds in S, what are the Ô¨Āelds in ¬ĮS?
Your Ô¨Ārst guess might be that E is the spatial part of one 4-vector and B the
spatial part of another. But your guess would be wrong‚ÄĒit‚Äôs more complicated
than that. Let me begin by making explicit an assumption that was already used
implicitly in Sect. 12.3.1: Charge is invariant. Like mass, but unlike energy, the
charge of a particle is a Ô¨Āxed number, independent of how fast it happens to be
moving. We shall assume also that the transformation rules are the same no matter
how the Ô¨Āelds were produced‚ÄĒelectric Ô¨Āelds associated with changing magnetic
Ô¨Āelds transform the same way as those set up by stationary charges. Were this
not the case we‚Äôd have to abandon the Ô¨Āeld formulation altogether, for it is the
essence of a Ô¨Āeld theory that the Ô¨Āelds at a given point tell you all there is to know,
electromagnetically, about that point; you do not have to append extra information
regarding their source.
With this in mind, consider the simplest possible electric Ô¨Āeld: the uni-
form Ô¨Āeld in the region between the plates of a large parallel-plate capacitor
(Fig. 12.35a). Say the capacitor is at rest in S0 and carries surface charges ¬ĪŌÉ0.
